sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with a 2-week history of right shoulder pain, exacerbated by physical activity, particularly lifting and raising his arm.
The pain is located on the outside of the shoulder and is tender to touch.
The patient reports no restriction in arm movement, but experiences pain with movement.
The pain is intermittent and exacerbated by nighttime sleeping on the affected side.
The patient has taken Advil and applied ice to alleviate the pain.
The patient has a history of occasional shoulder pain, dating back to a baseball injury in his youth.
He denies numbness in his arm or hands.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of a shoulder injury from playing baseball in his youth.
Review of systems: The patient denies numbness in his arm or hands.
Current medications: Advil for pain relief.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 116/84, heart rate 62, respirations 16, oxygen saturation 99%, temperature 97.8 degrees Fahrenheit.

Physical exam: Limited active and passive range of motion of the right shoulder, tenderness at the greater tuberosity of the humerus, no tenderness at the sternoclavicular or acromioclavicular joints.
Good hand grip and intact sensation to touch on neurovascular exam of the right arm.
Capillary refill less than 3 seconds.
Test Results: No fracture on shoulder x-ray.
MRI ordered to rule out other conditions.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has rotator cuff tendinopathy, likely due to overuse from recent yard work projects.
Problem list: Rotator cuff tendinopathy; possible overuse injury; no other problems mentioned.
Plan:
I plan to refer the patient to physical therapy for 6-8 weeks to address the rotator cuff tendinopathy.
I will continue to monitor the patient's symptoms and adjust the treatment plan as necessary.
I will also order an MRI to rule out other conditions.
The patient will continue to take Tylenol for pain relief.
If the symptoms do not improve, we may consider a steroid injection to the shoulder.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up with me in 6-8 weeks to re-evaluate the effectiveness of the physical therapy.
